schools.summary: Against the mystery of godliness, the dark prophecy — 'the Spirit speaketh expressly' (rhetos, plainly, sure revelation not conjecture); 'in the latter times' (the gospel age, ascension to judgment, Wesley); 'some shall depart from the faith' (apostatize) 'giving heed to seducing spirits and doctrines of devils' (error inspired from below); the sharp contrast (Ellicott) just after the glorious mystery.

The human instruments — the apostasy comes 'through the hypocrisy of men that speak lies' (deceivers teaching falsehood under a mask of piety that lends their errors credit); 'having their conscience seared with a hot iron' (kekausteriasmenon, cauterized, branded and deadened past feeling, the moral sense destroyed, Wesley); hypocritical deceit without and a deadened conscience within.

themes: conscience seared with a hot iron

schools.summary: The apostasy's ascetic marks — 'forbidding to marry' (condemning marriage as unholy, the Essenes and Gnostics counting celibacy higher sanctity; Clarke applies it to forbidding clergy to wed) and 'commanding to abstain from meats' (avoidance of foods as defiling, cf Col 2:21); refuted — these God 'created to be received with thanksgiving,' his good gifts; the false teachers slandering the Creator, calling unclean what he made good.

themes: created to be received with thanksgiving

schools.summary: The principle against asceticism — 'every creature of God is good' (everything he made, echoing Gen 1:31's 'very good'; the created order, food and marriage, not evil), 'nothing to be refused' (no warrant for ascetic taboos), 'if received with thanksgiving' (taken gratefully from God's hand); to teach any created thing unclean insults the Creator (Ellicott); it is contempt of the gifts, not their use, that offends.

themes: every creature of God is good

schools.summary: The gifts 'sanctified' (hagiazetai, rendered holy for use) by two means — 'the word of God' (which pronounced all good and gave them to man, ref. Gen 1, declaring them clean) and 'prayer' (the believer's thanksgiving and blessing over the gift, grace at the table consecrating common food); the word that grants and the prayer that blesses setting the gift apart; not abstinence but grateful, prayerful use.

themes: sanctified by the word of God

schools.summary: The good minister marked by faithful instruction — 'put the brethren in remembrance of these things' (reminding them of the truth, esp. against the false asceticism; not inventing novelties); 'a good minister of Jesus Christ' (faithful warning and teaching, diligent, establishing in the old truth, Henry); 'nourished up in the words of faith' (himself fed on sound doctrine, feeding others from the store that feeds him).

themes: a good minister of Jesus Christ
nourished in the words of faith

schools.summary: Fables against godliness — 'refuse' (paraitou, beg off from) 'profane and old wives' fables' (graodeis mythous, silly unholy myths — the false teachers' speculations, Jewish lore, later legend; common and beneath a minister); 'exercise thyself' (the athletic figure — train, discipline, labour, as for the games) 'rather unto godliness' (the cultivation of true piety, with the utmost labour, Wesley).

'Bodily exercise profiteth little' (somatike gymnasia, the athletic training, or the ascetics' bodily austerities of v3 — small or short-lived value, 'for a little'); 'godliness is profitable unto all things' (useful in every respect, body and soul, time and eternity), 'having promise of the life that now is, and of that which is to come' (God's blessing in both worlds); answering the false asceticism that made bodily severity the height of religion.

themes: promise of this life and the next

schools.summary: 'A faithful saying and worthy of all acceptation' — the solemn formula (as 1 Tim 1:15) marking a truth of the first importance, trustworthy and to be received without reservation; here pointing back to the surpassing profit of godliness (Robertson); a recognized watchword already of broad currency in the early churches (Ellicott), whose truth none need doubt (Clarke).

The ground of apostolic toil — 'therefore' (for godliness' sake) 'we both labour and suffer reproach' (toil and endure scorn for the godliness that profits unto all things), 'because we trust in the living God' (hope fixed on him who can make good his promise of life); 'Saviour of all men, specially of those that believe' — not universal salvation but God the Preserver/benefactor of all, and Saviour in the full redeeming sense especially of believers (Wesley, Gill).

debates.question: How is God 'the Saviour of all men'?

sides.position: the Preserver and benefactor of all (providential), and Saviour in the full redeeming sense 'specially of those that believe' — not universal salvation

schools.summary: The twofold charge — 'command' (enjoin with authority, enforce as binding) and 'teach' (instruct, make plain) 'these things' (the truths just stated — God's good gifts, the profit of godliness, hope in the living God), the substance of Timothy's preaching (Clarke); the minister teaching by life as well as doctrine (Henry).
Chrysostom: some matters require command, others teaching; to command where one should teach, or teach where one should command, is to misjudge.

Youth overcome by example — 'let no man despise thy youth' (Timothy young for so weighty a charge, to give no occasion); the remedy 'be thou an example of the believers' (a pattern to copy, earning respect not demanding it); the spheres covering the whole man — 'word' (speech), 'conversation' (conduct), 'charity' (love), 'spirit' (temper), 'faith' (fidelity), 'purity'; the young minister acting with the gravity his office requires (Clarke).

themes: let no man despise thy youth

schools.summary: The standing ministerial duties 'till I come' — 'reading' (proseche, keep putting the mind on; the public reading of Scripture, chiefly the OT, Clarke, and private study), 'exhortation' (applying the word to the conscience, urging to duty), 'doctrine' (teaching sound truth); the staple of public ministry — the word read, applied, taught; 'expect no end without the means' (Wesley).

'Neglect not the gift' (charisma, the spiritual endowment for ministry — feeding, ruling, teaching — God's bestowal, not natural talent; not to be let lie unused, Wesley); 'given by prophecy' (prophetic direction marking him for office) 'with the laying on of the hands of the presbytery' (his ordination, the elders and Paul himself, 2 Tim 1:6, setting him apart); the gift to be actively used, cultivated, not let grow cold.

themes: laying on of hands of the presbytery

schools.summary: Wholehearted devotion — 'meditate' (tauta meleta, be diligent in, ponder and practise until they shape his conduct) 'upon these things'; 'give thyself wholly to them' (en toutois isthi, be absorbed and immersed, the ministry his one great business; he who is wholly in these is little in worldly things, Wesley); 'that thy profiting may appear to all' (visible growth commending the minister and answering his youth).

The climactic charge — 'take heed unto thyself' (his soul, character, life, the inward grace and holy walk) 'and unto the doctrine' (his teaching, pure and sound); the two chief pastoral requisites, both to be guarded, a flaw in either ruining the ministry (Ellicott); 'continue in them' (persevere steadfastly to the end); 'thou shalt save thyself and them that hear thee' (not the author but, under God, the appointed means of his own and his hearers' salvation).

themes: take heed to thyself and the doctrine
